data.title: 6 Barbell Stocks: Growth Meets Income For U.S. And Iran Negotiations
data.description:
Markets Stabilize, But Uncertainty Calls for Diversification

Markets have found short-term relief with the two-week ceasefire in the Iran war, easing immediate geopolitical risk, and helping to stabilize investor sentiment. Combined with what is expected to be a mostly positive quarterly earnings season, the current backdrop could support select stocks in the near term, especially those with solid fundamental strength and growth potential. Friday’s slightly softer than expected CPI also helps the case for growth stocks. However, the broader macro environment remains fragile as the ultimate resolution of the Iran war is still uncertain and oil supply disruptions can cause ongoing negative ripple effects, keeping gas prices and inflation elevated. Midterm elections can also add another layer of uncertainty, potentially driving further market swings in the months ahead.

Mixing Semis, Energy, Industrials and Real Estate With Oil

In this environment, where upside catalysts coexist with persistent downside risks, a barbell approach has worked well for us in the past. A mix that blends high-risk growth with lower-risk, yield-producing, defensive positions can achieve a strong barbell portfolio. This offers a way to participate in gains while reducing overall volatility when combined into a portfolio.

Best Stocks to Buy Now: A Barbell Approach

To select the best stocks to feature in this article, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and targeted the highest Quant-Rated Strong Buy stocks in the respective sector targets. The sectors include technology, energy, infrastructure-focused industrials, and real estate:

  • High-quality tech captures upside from earnings growth strength and AI-driven demand.
  • Energy provides a hedge against geopolitical risk and inflation while offering high yields.
  • Industrials add earnings visibility through backlog-driven demand.
  • REITs offer rate-sensitive upside and income, benefiting from stabilizing rates and resilient property-level cash flows.

Together, they balance cyclical growth and defensive qualities, allowing investors to navigate volatility while remaining positioned for multiple market outcomes.

1. Sandisk Corporation (SNDK)

  • Market Capitalization: $125.69B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 530

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 26

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage, and Peripherals

A holding in my Pro Quant Portfolio, Sandisk is an American multinational technology company operating in the data storage segment of the semiconductor industry, benefiting from growth demand for NAND flash driven by AI workloads, cloud computing, and enterprise storage needs. As a high-beta tech stock, it fits firmly into the growth side of the barbell strategy. In the current macro environment, where earnings strength can offset geopolitical uncertainty, Sandisk's exposure to data growth provides a strong tailwind.

Despite a stunning near 2,228% price jump over the past year, SNDK still amazingly has an attractive valuation when factoring in its forward growth. The company’s forward non-GAAP P/E of 20.91 offers more than a 6% discount to the sector median, while its forward PEG of 0.09 reveals a 93% discount to sector peers. Like many of the high-beta stocks I spotlight, SNDK’s attractive valuation can be justified by key metrics that give it an 'A+' grade for its Quant Growth Factor, but the growth story is told through Sandisk’s incredible margin expansion, which is expected to continue.

In its Q2 fiscal year 2026 earnings report, Sandisk crushed estimates as non-GAAP EPS of $6.20 beat by $2.66 and revenue of $3.03B (+61.2% Y/Y) beat by $340M. Expectations are for more of the same for the next quarter’s earnings to be reported April 30. SNDK’s growth story and momentum make it a prime example of a recovery pick that enhances its appeal for capturing risk-on sentiment when it returns in full force. From this high-growth starting point, the portfolio turns toward another semiconductor name with even greater leverage to AI-driven demand.

2. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU)

  • Market Capitalization: $475.35B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 3 out of 530

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 69

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

Also a PQP holding and in my Alpha Picks portfolio, Micron Technology designs and manufactures memory and storage solutions, positioning it at the center of the AI and data center buildout. Its DRAM and NAND products are critical to AI’s high performance demands, giving it strong earnings visibility as the hyperscale buildout continues. Within the barbell framework, Micron combines high-beta exposure with strong fundamentals, particularly through upward earnings revisions and expanding profit margins. From a Quant perspective, those expanding margins enable the earnings growth that in turn creates an attractive valuation profile.

Starting with MU’s forward non-GAAP P/E of 7.29x earnings, investors are getting a 67.31% discount to the sector median. After factoring in its forward growth, the 0.06 PEG reveals more than a 95% discount to sector peers. With DRAM and NAND shortages expected into 2027, MU is in a good position to continue expanding margins that support its forward growth, which earns MU an A+ Factor Grade.

With forward EPS Growth expected at more than 415% and Operating Cash Flow Growth at more than 128%, Micron’s ability to scale remains in place as demand from giants like Nvidia (NVDA) will only grow, further amplifying the factors driving margin growth. As markets weigh earnings strength against geopolitical and inflation risks, MU offers a powerful growth driver, which is balanced next by a stock that can benefit directly from those same macro pressures.

3. Riley Exploration Permian (REPX)

  • Market Capitalization: $843.72M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 19 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 3 out of 68

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Exploration and Production

Riley Exploration Permian is an upstream oil and gas company domestically focused on production in the Permian Basin, which is located in the southwestern region of the U.S. Its direct exposure to oil prices makes REPX a natural hedge against geopolitical instability and supply disruptions associated with ongoing tensions in the Middle East. Although some investors may prefer large-cap energy companies like Chevron (CVX) or Exxon Mobil (XOM), Riley Exploration Permian stands to benefit more from higher energy prices, which can come with inflation and global uncertainty. Strong free cash flow, a 4.09% yield, and strong recent performance momentum enhance its appeal. Topping off the attraction from a Quant perspective, I like what I see in the company’s key valuation metrics, which tell me there’s room to run for the stock price.

While REPX fits in our barbell mix as an income-producing oil price and inflation hedge, it’s also a growth story that begins with an attractive valuation. The company’s forward non-GAAP P/E of 7.45 offers a 45% discount to the sector median, while its growth-adjusted PEG ratio supports that valuation as it offers more than a 47% discount to sector peers. While REPX’s 'C+' Growth Factor grade may appear lackluster on the surface, the forward trend looks healthy.

The growth grade for REPX has improved from a ‘D-’ to a ‘C+’ over the past three months. Helping to explain this improvement is a positive transition from shrinking revenue (-4.44% YoY) to Forward Revenue projections of 12.40%, which is 170% ahead of the sector median. Supporting this growth, Riley Permian forecasts more than 20% oil volume growth in 2026 while accelerating capital deployment and buybacks. While growth stocks can thrive in a risk-on environment, REPX can provide balance by performing when those conditions deteriorate, which leads us to another energy stock that can supplement this portion of our barbell.

4. SM Energy Company (SM)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.75B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 28 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 7 out of 68

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Exploration and Production

SM Energy is a U.S.-based exploration and production company focused on oil-rich assets in the Permian Basin and Eagle Ford, positioning it to directly benefit from higher oil prices. In the current macro environment, marked by geopolitical uncertainty around the Iran war and ongoing supply disruptions, SM can benefit from wider margins amid elevated oil prices. The company also has solid fundamentals, generating strong free cash flow, which enables it to offer a nice yield of 3.11%. From a Quant perspective, SM stands out with an attractive valuation and rapidly improving growth and profitability factors (moving from 'D+' to 'B' and 'D-' to 'B' for its respective grades).

To highlight the company’s growth improvement, forward Revenue Growth is expected at over 39%, which is more than double its YoY growth. Forward EBITDA Growth is 35.40%, which is nearly 593% higher than the sector median. Shifting over to its profitability strength, SM’s EBITDA Margin of nearly 73% is significantly more than double the company’s sector peers.

This combination of improving fundamentals and macro tailwinds makes SM a compelling addition to the barbell’s more aggressive side while still serving the income generation and inflationary hedge roles. From energy’s cyclical upside, we now turn to an industrial stock that offers more stable, backlog-driven infrastructure growth potential.

5. Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL)

  • Market Capitalization: $13.37B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 23 out of 610

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 2 out of 36

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

Another Alpha Pick holding, Sterling Infrastructure is an engineering and construction company focusing on infrastructure, e-infrastructure, and transportation projects. Its exposure to data center development, power infrastructure, and public works creates a unique hybrid profile within the barbell strategy. Backlog-driven revenue growth provides earnings visibility, while electrification trends and AI power demand support long-term growth. Unlike traditional cyclicals, Sterling’s business is less dependent on consumer spending, making it more resilient in uncertain economic conditions.

Sterling is a growth story, and its 2026 potential is supported by management’s expectation to reach 25% revenue growth for the year as backlog and data center demand surge. Supportive Quant metrics include forward EBIT Growth of more than 30%, which is more than 3x the sector median, and forward EPS Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) at 19%, offering a 54.38% premium to sector peers. Meanwhile, Sterling’s sector-leading return on capital of over 19% indicates the company is doing an outstanding job of turning invested capital into profits relative to its peers.

Sterling’s combination of growth and profit stability helps bridge the gap between the portfolio’s higher-risk and defensive components, making it the perfect way to tie up our broader barbell strategy of balancing opportunity with protection.

6. DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.07B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 4 out of 170

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 17

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

DiamondRock Hospitality Company is a lodging REIT focused on premium hotels and resorts in top U.S. markets. This positions DRH to benefit from continued strength from more affluent consumers that have held up travel demand and support pricing power. Unlike some traditional REITs that are facing challenges, hospitality remains strong in the consumer segment. As I mentioned in a recent article, Top 3 REITs For Reliable Income In Volatile Markets, the political and economic backdrop warrants caution for the real estate sector as a whole. However, DRH sees several favorable factors supporting a positive outlook in 2026, including key markets hosting the majority of FIFA World Cup matches, America 250 celebrations, and post-renovation tailwinds.

DRH’s healthy 'B-' Valuation Factor grade is supported by multiple favorable metrics, especially its forward P/AFFO of 10.06, which offers nearly a 35% discount to the sector median. This provides evidence that DRH is trading well below the value of its cash flows, offering an element of stability for investors. This discounted valuation also leaves room for price appreciation as sentiment improves when rates stabilize. As a result, investors can benefit from DRH’s solid 3.97% yield, as supported by its 'A+' Dividend Safety Grade.

In the current macro environment, DRH adds a rate-sensitive element to the barbell mix, as REIT valuations tend to improve when interest rates stabilize or decline. The combination of potential upside and rate sensitivity makes DRH an addition to the mix, rounding out the portfolio before we turn to the broader conclusion.

Conclusion: Barbell Offers Best Stocks for "Time In" Markets

In a market driven by geopolitical uncertainty, inflation pressures, and shifting interest rate expectations, a barbell strategy offers a disciplined way to navigate volatility. Rather than timing the market, this approach enables "time in" the market by remaining exposed with a diversified mix. By combining high-growth semiconductor stocks with energy exposure, infrastructure industrials, and select REITs, this portfolio offers multiple ways to win with balance. Each position serves a role, whether through earnings-driven upside, macro hedging, income stability, or rate sensitivity. Rather than relying on a single market outcome, this approach allows investors to remain flexible, participating in gains while maintaining resilience in a range of economic scenarios.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Subscribe to Premium for unlimited access to Seeking Alpha quant ratings and expert content. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Feel free to check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

data.title: Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical: The Market Is Overlooking 2026 Catalysts
data.description:
It has been nearly a year and a half since my last Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical (RARE) article, where I discussed the company’s Q3 earnings report that showed a 42% year-over-year revenue increase, which encouraged me to finally commit to a starter position in RARE. I believed that the company’s late-stage pipeline could provide multiple catalysts for future growth, including Setrusumab and DTX401. At that time, Ultragenyx had a strong cash position, and I saw a path to profitability by 2026, which could allow the company to avoid the need for extensive dilutive financing. Since then, RARE is down roughly 50% after the company received a complete response letter (CRL) from the FDA for UX111 in Sanfilippo syndrome type A, then Setrusumab for osteogenesis imperfecta failed to hit the primary endpoint in two late-stage trials, and the company’s guidance fell short of the consensus. As a result, I decided to hold off on adding to my position.

However, I was reading a recent investors conference transcript and realized that my Ultragenyx thesis is still intact, as the company leverages their commercial products (Crysvita, Dojolvi, Mepsevii, and Evkeeza) to provide a financial base to help fund their impressive late-stage pipeline that could move the company closer to profitability. It appears that 2026 is going to be a pivotal year for Ultragenyx with multiple data readouts, regulatory milestones, and potential approvals that could bolster investor sentiment and improve RARE’s long-term prospects. As a result, I’m keeping a close eye on the ticker for an opportunity to add to my dormant position.

I intend to review the company’s current commercial foundation and the list of potential catalysts on the horizon. Then, I will take a look at the commercial implications of these programs and their potential impact on the company’s valuation. In addition, I will point out a few downside risks that investors should be aware of when managing their RARE position. Finally, I take a look at the daily chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to add to my position.

Underrated Commercial Foundation

When catching up on Ultragenyx’s current condition, I was really surprised that a rare disease biotech pulled in $673M last year, which was up 20% from 2024. Digging deeper, it looks as if Crysvita was responsible for $481M of that $673M. Meanwhile, Dojolvi brought in $96M, Mepsevii $37M, and Evkeeza experienced 84% growth year-over-year to log $59M.

So, I would say that Ultragenyx is capable of handling multiple launches in different rare disease arenas while still pushing their late-stage programs closer to the finish line. This commercial groundwork could provide enough stability for the company to make their push towards their goal of profitability by 2027.

Catalyst Timeline

Ultragenyx has several potent clinical catalysts slated in 2026 and 2027, including readouts from their Angelman syndrome program, Glycogen Storage Disease Type Ia (GSDIa), and Sanfilippo syndrome, as well as Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency and Wilson’s disease.

GTX-102 is Ultragenyx’s Angelman syndrome program and is one of their most anticipated catalysts, with two Phase III trials. It is important to note that Angelman syndrome does not have an approved therapy that has disease-modifying ability. So, there is a big opportunity for Ultragenyx to be the first mover and expand the market. In fact, one analyst believes that GTX-102 could have peak sales of $1.2B.

DTX401 is Ultragenyx’s gene therapy for GSDIa, which has a PDUFA date set in August 2026 after their Phase III trial showed improved glucose control and reduced dietary dependence. A Bank of America analyst has DTX401’s peak sales at about $796M, which would be impressive for therapy coming from a population of roughly 6K patients.

Ultragenyx’s UX111 for Sanfilippo syndrome could be another breakthrough drug because there is a lack of approved therapies. UX11’s cognitive and biomarker marks still have my approval. The company announced that the FDA has set a PDUFA date for September 19th, 2026.

The company’s DTX301 for the OTC program targets a rare urea cycle disorder, while UX701 targets Wilson’s disease. Both programs are in Phase III or are in pre-pivotal stages, with potentially important readouts over the next 12 months.

I would suggest reviewing the point above quickly one more time and understanding that we are looking at multiple Ultragenyx programs that are approaching FDA approval with breakthrough therapy designations. So, we could see Ultragenyx report approval across multiple programs, which could lead to multiple launches over the next few years, resulting in significant revenue growth going into the next decade.

Conservative Commercial Implications

I will concede that attempting to determine peak sales for rare disease and gene therapy products is incredibly murky because we don't really know what the market is for the product until it actually hits the market. We can look at patient populations and the market size to estimate the commercial opportunity, but penetration and prices are always a big question mark because these therapies are novel, so we don't know if there is demand for patients to try something completely new, and we don't know if payers are going to fully support the therapy. So, I tend to be cautious when trying to determine the valuation of rare disease pipelines.

I’m going to attempt to calculate very conservative peak estimates for Ultragenyx’s late-stage programs.

When trying to determine Ultragenyx’s late-stage pipeline valuation, I will be modeling using only the U.S. market with a 30% peak penetration and a conservative estimate of pricing based on other established rare-disease products. Under this conservative approach, I calculated that Ultragenyx has an aggregate $1.5B-$2.4B in peak annual revenue potential from these programs in the United States alone. So, not counting for ex-U.S. expansion or a broader label penetration.

GTW-102 for Angelman syndrome looks to be the largest single opportunity in the U.S. pipeline. With an estimated 6K to 10K treatable patients when I adjust for clinical eligibility constraints and only 30% peak penetration at an estimated $300K per patient (Spinraza is around $350K per year), I get a peak revenue range of $540M to $900M.

The second-largest opportunity could be DTX301 with OTC deficiency, with 800 to 1.2K treatable U.S. patients, and assuming a $1.5M one-time gene therapy price (Zolgensma is $2.1M) and 30% penetration, which gives us peak sales of $360M to $540M. DTX401 could add $210M to $300M in U.S. peak sales, assuming 350 to 500 eligible patients and a $2M price tag. In addition, UX701 could give Ultragenyx 5K-8K treatable U.S. patients with chronic therapies around $125K per year, which would be about $190M-$300M in peak sales at 30% penetration.

UX111 for Sanfilippo syndrome might be the smallest in absolute U.S. population, but the lack of approved disease-modifying therapies could make it a lucrative opportunity for Ultragenyx. With approximately 250 to 600 eligible U.S. patients and applying 30% penetration with a $2M price tag (Zolgensma comp), we get a peak sales range from $150M to $360M.

I know these estimates aren't very rosy, but I'm deliberately modeling them with some bearish assumptions. I did this to illustrate that Ultragenyx still has a lot to gain even if all of these programs achieve modest penetration, which could still yield a multi-billion-dollar expansion in their revenue base over time.

I’m not the only one with an optimistic outlook. It appears Street analysts are also expecting the company to report strong double-digit growth over the next 10 years, going from roughly $770M in 2026 to about $3.75B in 2035.

What is more, Street analysts are projecting Ultragenyx to report similar EPS growth, going from -4.40 in 2026 to 12.21 in 2035.

The industry’s average price-to-sales is roughly 4x-5x, so assuming Ultragenyx were to pull in roughly $3.75B in 2035, we could be looking at about $152 per share. RARE is currently trading around $23 per share, so there is significant upside if all goes well for Ultragenyx over the next 10 years.

Risks to Consider

Certainly, Ultragenyx has significant downside risks to consider when managing your position. I would first highlight the hallmark risks that come with being a rare disease company. We have to recognize that some of these programs are targeting very small patient populations, so a small network of treatment centers might impact how quickly patients can get to a center and be treated. So, it is possible that we see some of these programs get off to a slow start, despite there being a clear need.

Another risk to consider is that some of Ultragenyx’s programs are gene therapies, which carry plenty of risks, including the complexity of administration. AAV-based gene therapies often require the patient to go through pre-screening, preconditioning of their immune system, and post-treatment monitoring. Just adding these steps could limit site capacity and throughput. So, even if there is demand, it is possible that Ultragenyx’s therapies won't be able to scale fast enough early in the commercial launch.

We also have to acknowledge that the rare disease addressable market can be impacted by a lag in diagnosis and referrals. As a result, the real-world treatable population could be much smaller than the estimated market derived from prevalence statistics. The company can launch education and support programs to help improve genetic testing and newborn screening, but they are still relying on other parties to execute.

Perhaps the most concerning is the payer dynamics of rare diseases, which can add a lot of friction. Even when we are dealing with orphan indications, payers appear to be reluctant to really support these therapies. Yes, rare disease therapies pose a big risk for payers because they are flipping the bill for therapies with big price tags but limited real-world data. So, even if Ultragenyx gets these therapies approved, payers might require a prior authorization, ask for restrictive eligibility, and require a reimbursement agreement. Any of those hurdles could delay access to the company’s therapies and hurt the early stages of a commercial launch.

We also have to acknowledge the potential threat from competition. Although most of Ultragenyx’s programs have limited competition, we have to anticipate that a competitor will challenge these products at some point down the road. This could force Ultragenyx to adjust their prices substantially, which might result in a significant loss in revenue.

I also want to stress that having so many potent catalysts on the docket could lead to elevated volatility as the market attempts to digest the likelihood of approval and commercial success. RARE has a history of the share price being essentially cut in half following weak data or regulatory setbacks. So, investors need to accept that their position could have big swings in value over the next couple of years as the company’s current pipeline attempts to get through the FDA. Keep in mind that any setback or failure could generate doubts about the company’s financial health and timeline to break even. The company did finish 2025 with $680M in cash, but that cash position could quickly evaporate as the company maintains several late-stage programs and commercial launch prep. Plus, the company has $1.25B in debt, so the threat of dilution could grow if the company doesn’t have a strong winning percentage over the next 2 years.

Last but not least, Ultragenyx might have to deal with regulatory uncertainty and macroeconomic factors. Healthcare costs continue to rise, and scrutiny of the regulatory approval process for novel treatment options might lead to harsh ramifications for Ultragenyx's future operations.

Despite these risks, I'm maintaining my RARE conviction rating at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare "Bio Boom" speculative portfolio

My Plan

As I mentioned in my introduction, my RARE position has been gathering dust for over a year now, but the recent sell-off has enticed me to consider picking up some shares at these prices ahead of some potent catalysts.

Looking at the RARE Daily Chart, we see a potential double-bottom setup forming along with a bullish divergence on the RSI while showing strength. Plus, we can see the share price has climbed above the proximal anchored-VWAP and is now neutral on the Go-No-Go indicator while pressing the top of the Keltner Channel.

Indeed, the share price will still have to deal with the 200-Day EMA, and the longstanding anchored VWAP above, but I would say some bullish signals are starting to light up. So, I will look to add in the immediate term.

Once I have added, I will switch up my RARE game plan to focus on catalyst-driven opportunities to manage my position.

Admittedly, I will not hesitate to book profits on spikes in the share price following clinical or commercial updates. My goal is to trade this position in order to generate profit and construct a "house money" core position that has been funded by the market to hold as a long-term investment.

data.title: Gambling.com: SEO Recovery Appears Underway
data.description:
It's been more than a few months since my last article on Gambling.com Group (GAMB), and quite a few developments have occurred:

  • FY-25 results
  • CEO change
  • SEO recovery (partially)
  • Insider buys

We will discuss all of these developments in the article, but I want to first highlight how disconnected GDG's stock is from reality. The company's market cap currently stands at $125 million. For perspective, the company's historical and projected FCFs are as follows:

  • 2021: $13.7 million
  • 2022: $18.4 million
  • 2023: $17.5 million
  • 2024: $36.3 million
  • 2025: $36.3 million
  • 2026: $39.6 million (expected based on last year's FCF margin and 2026 revenue guidance)

In other words, if the company stopped growing today, it could theoretically buy back every single share in three years. That said, readers familiar with the company know there are meaningful debt burdens preventing GDG from executing large buybacks in the near term (2026-2028). Still, the point stands that the company is being valued as if it is going out of business, when in reality, revenue and profits are hitting record highs each year. The company's FCF makes up such a substantial portion of its market cap that it is truly unbelievable to me how the price can be so low.

Management appears to agree. The (now former) CEO, Charles Gillespie, recently disclosed that insiders purchased over 500k shares in the open market, representing roughly 1.5% of the total float, with him leading the charge, buying 351k of those shares.

At the end of the day, GDG is a company growing at double digits with double-digit FCF margins. Conservatively forecasting GDG's future FCF leads me to believe the company is worth about $17 per share.

Current State of the Business

Rather than rehash numbers, I want to focus on a higher level. We will also start with the bear case.

GDG enters 2026 as a business in transition, with two distinct segments: marketing and sports data. The marketing side of the business has come under significant (although 'overblown' is a better word) pressure, specifically in regard to SEO marketing. Organic search, historically a high-margin growth driver, has become less reliable due to a combination of Google algorithm instability, increased competition (including AI-driven spam), and evolving user behavior. Former CEO Charles Gillespie repeatedly described this as "business as usual," expecting SEO performance to normalize in subsequent quarters.

That quarter never came.

I wouldn't say there is a structural impairment in GDG's SEO channel yet, but if this trend continues throughout 2026, I might concede. That said, while SEO has struggled, management has done an exceptional job scaling alternative paid marketing channels to offset lost revenue. They don't get enough credit for the speed at which this was executed. To put this into perspective, marketing revenue still grew while organic traffic on some flagship domains declined by as much as 80% YoY. The tradeoff, however, is margin compression, as paid channels are structurally lower margin than SEO (gross margins declined to 91% in 2025 vs. 94% in 2024 and 100% in years past).

The other segment, sports data, is both highly profitable and growing, but it is essentially an acquired business following GDG's $160 million purchase of Odds Holdings in 2025. GDG would like you to believe this is the future of the company, which could ultimately prove true, but at this moment in time, sports data is not scaling anywhere near the level required to justify its acquisition price or investor belief that it's the future of the company.

Odds Holding makes up 25% of GDG revenue and is only growing in the high teens, which is less than marketing's historical growth, meaning that sports data can mathematically never make up more than 25% of GDG revenue unless marketing declines significantly or sports data experiences exponential growth, for which 'high teens' does not qualify.

Turning to the balance sheet, we can see a picture of a highly leveraged company. The company possesses $16.4 million in cash and $192 million in liabilities. While this appears insurmountable at first glance, the company's FCF is sufficient enough to deleverage the balance sheet over 3-5 years.

Future State of the Business

Not all is woe at GDG. In fact, there is an immense potential upside if the business continues to stabilize and even resumes moderate growth.

Here are some positives:

  1. For the first time in the company's history, SEO revenue made up less than half of overall marketing revenue, signaling less dependence on one channel.
  2. When accounting for marketing revenue shares and sports data subscriptions, over 50% of GDG's revenue is recurring.
  3. Prediction markets offer a potential growth avenue as they significantly expand the TAM across both marketing and sports data. Prediction markets create new demand for odds data, trading tools, and consumer education.
  4. In the year the company's search traffic collapsed, management still grew the marketing segment. At this point, the worst is past us, as there is little search traffic left to lose.

For 2026, the company expects revenue in the range of $170 million to $180 million, representing 3-9% YoY growth. My gut feeling is management sandbagged guidance to avoid their Q3-25 fiasco, where they had to cut guidance down from $175 million to $165 million, resulting in a huge sell-off of the stock.

When asked why guidance appears so subdued, CEO Charles Gillespie effectively admitted the company is prioritizing conservatism, stating they are "very conscious of putting out numbers that we can hit even if none of those things outperform," and later adding, "That's the idea… we want to be pretty conservative."

Speaking of Charles Gillespie, GDG has a new CEO at the helm: co-founder Kevin McCrystle. The transition takes place this May, after 20 years of CEO service from Gillespie, who will become appointed Executive Chairman of the Board. I think it's great the new CEO is the co-founder, who, unlike an external CEO, has much more natural motivation to continue growing the business.

Valuation

Let's take a look at a reasonable DCF, incorporating a few recent developments:

  • Partial SEO recovery
  • The World Cup
  • Prediction Markets

Revenue

I forecast 10% revenue growth for the next decade. This is essentially the top of their (sandbagged) guidance for 2026. I believe 10% is conservative for two reasons:

  1. The company's historical revenue growth rate is 40.73% over the past seven years (though primarily acquisition-driven).

  2. SEO trends appear to be stabilizing, which should support a return to marketing growth.

Partial SEO Recovery

GDG's flagship domain, gambling.com, experienced a massive decline in organic traffic during 2025, down roughly 80% since its peak in the summer of 2024. However, since the end of February 2026, organic traffic has shown some life again, as we can see in the above chart. Gambling.com hit a peak of 500k of organic traffic, and as former CEO Gillespie importantly stated, SEO conversion rates remained stable throughout the decline, meaning traffic recovery should translate directly into revenue recovery.

In some serious positive news, RotoWire appears to be killing it. Organic search traffic is at ATHs, crossing 7 million visitors, and recently, Craig Cummings, VP of GDG, posted that RotoWire is the biggest casino affiliate YTD 2026, according to The Business of iGaming.

As a general note, RotoWire revenue is captured in the sports data segment, not the marketing segment, but either way, this rapid growth should help contribute to revenue growth.

Looking ahead, GDG is likely to capture two large opportunities: the World Cup and prediction markets. The former should drive sports betting activity, while the latter expands demand for data products. GDG also became the first provider of proprietary sportsbook data API inside Claude. While this doesn't necessarily translate to immediate revenue growth, it does create a small moat and competitive advantage (first-mover advantage).

Note: Semrush seems to be more generous with the way it measures organic traffic. AhRefs is more conservative, but I don't have access to its data.

Operating Margins

I set operating margins to be 20% to reflect the increased cost of diversifying marketing channels. For perspective, the company's operating margins were 21.37% in 2025 and averaged 26.81% over the past nine years. When compared to the nine-year average, I essentially baked in a 25% margin of safety in the margins.

Other Assumptions

All other assumptions were set on historical averages, and the WACC was calculated to be 8.8%. The terminal rate is set to 2% to conservatively keep pace with inflation.

If my forecasts prove accurate, the stock is worth almost $17 per share, representing a 368% return from today's severely depressed levels of $3.61 per share.

Risks

Gambling.com Group carries numerous risks for potential investors. The following list is meant to highlight select important risks but is non-exhaustive.

Credibility & Execution Risk

Management has consistently guided towards a return to growth in the marketing segment, yet results have materially lagged those expectations. If management continues to misguide (i.e., with their late 2025 downward guidance revision), the more investors will lose faith in them.

In fact, I would argue that skepticism is already taking place as I write this, as it seems many funds have sold out of their positions and may not re-enter without proven clarity from management.

Unclear Near-Term Growth Drivers

State-level legalization of online gambling and sports betting is slow and unpredictable, thus making contributions from new markets sporadic. The market rewards consistency, and the natural unpredictability of new cash flows in this industry acts as a lid on the stock price. Additionally, the company's specific plan/strategy around capitalizing on prediction markets is unknown.

Google Dependency

Despite meaningful progress, GDG still services a significant portion of marketing revenue from Google-driven traffic. While this SEO dependency has declined to a little under half of marketing revenue, it remains a key risk. Continued volatility in search ranking and algorithm changes could materially impact performance until diversification efforts and the sports data segment represent a larger share of the business.

AI

There was speculation last year that AI would cannibalize a lot of Google search inquiries. However, this was proven false as Google search queries actually increased. I currently don't view AI as a threat to GDG, as they will continue to integrate APIs into LLMs, and the intent behind AI queries and Search queries is different. This is a rapidly evolving and dynamic space, though, and thus, this risk should be kept top of mind by investors.

Regulatory Risk

The online gambling industry is highly regulated (recent developments in Finland highlight how quickly policy shifts can impact companies such as Gambling.com). Any future tightening or changing of regulations could weigh on both revenue and profitability.

Swish Lawsuit

Another layer of uncertainty stems from the ongoing $100 million Swish Analytics litigation against Odds Holding related to data scraping. The outcome of the lawsuit, or any indemnification from the original owners of Odds Holding, is unclear and needs to be monitored. An adverse outcome would be a significant cash flow drain on GDG and could delay its share price appreciation.

Takeaway

GDG is a company that needs a few years to sort out its balance sheet and restore some confidence in its marketing business. During that time, if the company can continue growing the sports data segment, it should emerge in 2029 with a clean balance sheet, plenty of FCF, and thus, the ability to cannibalize its own shares.

I conservatively value the company at $17 based on the forecasts written in this piece. As always with an investment, investors must weigh opportunity cost, risk, and reward. In GDG's case, I believe the reward could be immense, but the opportunity cost of waiting three years and risk profile may not be preferential or suitable, respectively, for some investors.

data.title: Spartan Delta: Putting The Success Odds In Your Favor
data.description:
Many years ago, when I first began writing articles for the website, there was a huge interest in having me write about energy stocks that did not separate investors from their money, as is extremely common in this industry. The very extreme case was a stock I wrote about years ago that quadrupled in price one week before it filed for bankruptcy (roughly). The other thing I wrote about was "The Big Discovery Trap" which very often happens with very small companies. Spartan Delta (DALXF) has long been a way to avoid a lot of the "traps" in this industry, as the last article noted.

The Record

This management has long been known for building and then selling substantial parts of the company to essentially begin over again.

A company like this one is suitable for new investors in the energy industry because this management has a fairly long history of success, as shown above.

Probably the most interesting takeaway from managements like this one is that many investors believe that a lot of debt is necessary for a "homerun" in this part of the stock market. But most of the managements like this one that are very successful generally keep the debt levels to a very conservative level. Unlike a very common investor attitude, most managements consider debt a very seldom used tool.

But the major idea is that successful (or "great") investors often center upon repeatable results (and consistency). Whereas much of the time I run into the attitude of "How do I get every last penny?"

Rather than get every last penny from the current idea, I tend to look for results, as shown above, that are far more likely to avoid big losses.

Logan Energy

As is often the case with professionals that build and sell (either companies or parts of companies), this management decided that a certain proposal was different enough that a separate company, Logan Energy (LOECF) was formed and spun off. Both companies are Canadian operators that report using Canadian dollars. The only difference is the location chosen for operations.

Evidently, management considered the location considerations different enough to justify a separate company. But again, the same management shown above that made all those returns to shareholders also runs this company.

That puts the odds in favor of the investor when many similar-sized companies will not be around a few years later.

The Business

As was noted in the annual earnings press release in February, the remaining operations had the main goal of producing from an interval that produced rich gas. These leases had long produced dry gas. But newer production techniques due to advancing technology had now made a different interval competitive.

As a result of this shift in emphasis, oil production grew tremendously, as shown above. That is very likely to lower the corporate breakeven point in the long run as well as increase profitability.

Map Of Operations

This company is at the rapidly growing stage. Generally, whenever a decision is made to sell some operations or spin off some part of the company into a new company, there is a beginning budget made.

As of right now, there would appear to be a budget that will keep production level with the past fiscal year. But there is also a goal to increase the more valuable liquids production as the natural gas production decreases.

That means that even though production per share may not increase, profitability is very likely to increase.

It can be a bit scary to watch experienced management "slap a company together" rapidly because oftentimes, in the beginning, cash flow is very negative. But a management as successful as this one knows what it is doing and has a final goal in mind.

Most of these managements will switch to a "live within means" model that may or may not include a dividend in the future. Therefore, out-of-control cash spending is generally not the issue here as it is elsewhere.

Nonetheless, it does not hurt to watch the debt levels "just in case".

Summary

A company like Spartan Delta is one that is suitable for a wide variety of investors. However, new investors may want to focus on a company like this one to learn about the industry in a relatively safe way. This management is going to do a lot of things right that can then be used to compare against other industry ideas.

What further simplifies things is that generally a stock like this can be held until management sells the company. This helps beginning investors to determine when the time comes to buy the industry and then when to sell the industry.

When a management like this one jumps back into the industry (as several have in other articles I wrote), then obviously it is time to consider purchasing shares of companies. Similarly, when a management sells part or all of the company, as was done a couple of years back, then it is time to see if that was a sell signal by people that really know the industry.

In the rare event that the stock price clearly gets ahead of itself, it can be sold and then repurchased on the inevitable pullback. Right now, we seem to be far away from an overpriced stock, though.

The finances here are rock solid with a very conservative debt level. That is typical for a management with this level of experience.

Risks

A severe and sustained commodity price downturn might well cause me to re-evaluate the prospects of the company. But it should also be considered that the conservative debt level combined with management experience would likely cause a lot less suffering here than is the case elsewhere in the industry.

One of the reasons for the low debt levels is the very volatile stock prices. Future stock prices are also considered to have very low visibility. That makes financial leverage extra dangerous. Instead, a management like this is likely to go for an operational advantage by finding above-average geology to work with. Then the company will be far better suited to withstanding an extremely nasty cyclical downturn.

Similarly, there are many companies that announce a "big discovery". But North America is one of the most explored continents in the world. Therefore, the location of the oil is often known by the industry well ahead of any "discovery" announcement. Rather than relying upon reserves, investors need to concentrate on production costs, cash flow, and amounts recoverable. Often times, smaller companies' "big discovery announcements" are followed up with disappointing production results (and high costs).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Iran: Not Close To Over Yet
data.description:
Thanks, mostly to Wednesday's big rally, the major indices had their best week since November. The S&P 500 added 3.6% this week, the Nasdaq climbed 4.7%, and the Dow, advanced three percent. With last week's rise, all the major indexes are now close to where they started 2026, despite the huge uptick in volatility.

Last week's trading action was a great example of how complacent investors have become despite growing threats to the U.S. economy and equity markets. The big rise in stocks last week was triggered by a two-week ceasefire announced at the 11th hour Tuesday between the U.S. and Iran. To call this ceasefire 'fragile' is overly generous. The agreement supposedly consisted of a cessation of hostilities combined with the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

There is already a divergence of opinion between the U.S. and Iran on whether this ceasefire included Lebanon, where Israel has launched their biggest bombing missions in decades in order to root out Iran's proxy Hezbollah. And very few ships have gone through the Strait of Hormuz, with only a handful of Iran‑linked and/or Chinese vessels transiting this chokepoint. An insignificant number, given over 800-900 vessels are backed up waiting to go through this transit point. Roughly half of which are oil tankers.

And while talks in Pakistan will be the highest-level face-to-face talks between Tehran and Washington in years, given the apparent gulf between the two sides, it is hard to be overly optimistic of a permanent resolution being reached. My own view is that it is basically a coin flip which way these discussions play out.

I could easily see these discussions leading to nowhere. Both sides will use the two-week ceasefire to rearm, and hostilities will break out again as soon as the ceasefire period ends, if not before. And that scenario is not priced into the markets. Interestingly, the oil markets, after posting their biggest daily decline in six years on Wednesday, did little the rest of the week. One would expect oil would have plunged further if investors believed a long-term solution was in the offing. 10-year Treasury yields also should have dropped if there was optimism around peace talks, but that simply hasn't happened. Therefore, my view is markets believe this conflict isn't nearly over yet.

And even if peace broke out next week, a lot of damage has already been done to both the U.S. and global economy. It may be already too late to avoid a recession or at least a bout of stagflation. And these signs are increasingly present. March consumer sentiment plunged last week to historical lows, with one-year inflation expectations rising a full 100 bps from the previous month to 4.8%.

And speaking of fragile, there was a cacophony of voices noting the increasing probabilities of recession and/or stagflation for the U.S. economy last week. Mark Zandi, the chief economist at Moody’s Analytics, stated that recession risks have become “uncomfortably high” as the U.S. economy is now “very fragile” and teetering on the edge of a downturn. Mr. Zandi also unveiled a new recession indicator he dubbed the “Vicious Cycle Index,” which is currently already pointing to a recession. This indicator is basically the classic Sahm Rule modified to account for labor participation rates. Not as catchy as the 'Misery Index' developed during the last big bout of stagflation in the U.S. in the late 1970s, but a metric to keep an eye on.

Mr. Zandi was hardly the only pundit in the Stagflation corner last week. The IMF warned last week that the ongoing conflict in Iran will 'slow global growth, raise inflation, and worsen food insecurity globally.' Not to be outdone, the head of U.S. equity and quantitative strategy at BofA Securities noted that current market indicators pointed to a 'classic stagflationary market environment.'

Investors are already seeing the early signs of an inflationary surge. Friday's CPI reading rose 0.9% on a month-over-month basis in March. This was up from 0.3% in February. March's CPI level was up 3.3% over the prior 12 months. This was up significantly from 2.4% in February. Gasoline prices surged just over 21% during the month, which accounted for nearly three-quarters of the CPI increase from February.

Diesel prices have risen more than 50% so far in 2026. That increases logistical costs across the entire economy, as trucks and trains use diesel to move goods to market. The price of jet fuel in the U.S. has also roughly doubled. It has moved from $2.50 a gallon on Feb. 27 to $4.88 a gallon on April 2. It has fallen back a bit since then. United Airlines (UAL) and other airlines have raised fees to help offset costs and have started to raise fares. A jet fuel shortage could trigger mass cancellations in Europe if not relieved over the next three weeks.

In summary, I view last week's rise as little more than a short-lived relief rally. The probabilities of new hostilities breaking out in the Middle East by the end of April appear to be at least 50/50. The chances of the Strait of Hormuz being fully reopened are much, much lower than that. Simply put, this isn't over yet. Nowhere close, in fact. And the longer this global chokepoint remains effectively closed, the greater the likelihood of recession or stagflation.

Neither scenario is properly priced into the markets. Like most investors, I was happy to see green across most of my portfolio last week after some rough going for equities in March. That said, I did little trading within my portfolio. I rolled some of my covered call holdings forward and added a bit to my gold mining positions. At the end of the week, my portfolio was roughly 30% in short-term treasuries and cash and 70% in covered call holdings around the few stocks in the market I see as attractively or reasonably priced. If hostilities renew in the weeks ahead, there will be ample opportunities to use some of that 'dry powder' to act upon lower entry points in the market.

data.title: VICI Properties: A Winning High-Yield Bet To Buy Now
data.description:
In an environment of market volatility, many may feel forced to choose between the safety of high-yield income and the excitement of capital appreciation. In my view, the most resilient portfolios bridge this gap by selecting compounders. Businesses with a stable floor of cash flow and the infrastructure for future growth.

One opportunity sits at the intersection of trophy real estate and disciplined financial engineering. Sporting a perfect rent collection record and a multi-year path for growth, this investment-grade REIT is trading at a valuation that implies the market is overlooking its potential.

Of course, I’m talking about VICI Properties (VICI). When I last covered VICI with a Buy rating in December, I thought that the net lease REIT’s growth prospects were intact. VICI’s balance sheet was investment-grade. Shares were also a compelling value.

Four months later, I’m reiterating my Buy rating. Alongside its annual lease escalators, VICI’s expanded relationship with Cain and Eldridge Industries, as well as the acquisition of Pure Casino Entertainment’s assets, are tangible growth catalysts. VICI’s net leverage ratio was at the low end of its targeted leverage ratio. Finally, shares remain attractively valued.

More AFFO Growth to Come

On February 25th, VICI shared its earnings report for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company’s total revenue increased by 3.8% over the year-ago period to $1.01 billion in the quarter. Adjusting for a 1.2% increase in the share count, VICI’s revenue grew solidly at a low single-digit clip on a per-share basis during the quarter.

What contributed to the net lease REIT’s revenue growth for the fourth quarter? Most of VICI’s total revenue growth was made possible by a 6.7% uptick in income from lease financing receivables to $448.77 million in the quarter. Contributing factors behind this higher revenue included the initial $450 million mezzanine loan investments tied to the development of One Beverly Hills, a landmark 17.5-acre luxury mixed-use development in Beverly Hills, California, and the up to $510 million in delayed draw term loans for the development of the North Fork Mono Casino & Resort.

VICI’s income from sales-type leases edged 1.9% higher over the year-ago period to $534.65 million during the fourth quarter. That was due to contractual escalators embedded in VICI’s existing leases and reinvestment-driven rent growth at the Venetian Resort Las Vegas.

These more than offset slight declines in other income and golf revenue for VICI for the fourth quarter (down 3% to $18.88 million and 3.2% to $10.79 million, respectively).

The net lease REIT’s AFFO per share rose by 5.3% year-over-year to $0.60 in the fourth quarter. The combination of disciplined management of costs and $2.1 billion of capital commitments in 2025 helped VICI’s AFFO margin expand by 180 basis points to 63.4% during the quarter. This is how its AFFO per share growth outpaced revenue growth for the quarter.

Looking ahead, there are plenty of reasons to like VICI.

For one, 46% of the rent roll is subject to CPI-linked escalation in 2026. By 2035, this will rise to 90% of the rent roll. That provides a built-in floor for AFFO per share growth.

Then, there are the deals that VICI has been executing lately to drive further growth. Late last month, the company increased its One Beverly Hills Mezzanine loan to $1.5 billion alongside JPMorgan’s (JPM) $2.8 billion senior loan commitment. This is an additional $1.05 billion incremental commitment beyond VICI’s $450 million investment in the project. While not explicitly mentioned, the interest rate is likely in the range of 8% to 9%.

More importantly, this isn’t just a passive loan. The mezzanine loan gives VICI a front-row seat to eventually acquire the real estate of the Aman Beverly Hills and associated luxury retail/residential components if Cain and Eldridge ever seek to monetize the assets.

Another deal that VICI recently announced was a 200.6 million CAD ($144.4 million) purchase of the real estate assets of Deerfoot Inn & Casino, Great Northern Casino, and two limited-service hotels adjacent to the Great Northern Casino located in Alberta, Canada. This deal with Pure Casino Entertainment represents an 8% cap rate (with a 25-year initial base lease term and four five-year tenant renewal options). The portfolio rent will escalate at 1% on the first February 1st following a full 12 months post-closing, and the escalation thereafter will be the greater of 1.5% or the change in Canadian CPI (capped at 2.5%). Like the Golden Entertainment acquisition that I outlined in my previous article, this acquisition is also expected to close in mid-2026.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for AFFO per share to grow by 2.9% in 2026 to $2.45. In 2027, an additional 3.3% jump in AFFO per share to $2.53 is being projected. For 2028, another 3.2% increase in AFFO per share to $2.61 is the current consensus.

VICI is financially stable, too. As of December 31st, 2025, the net lease REIT’s LQA net leverage ratio was 5.0x. This is at the low end of its targeted net leverage ratio of between 5x and 5.5x. The debt maturity schedule is also well-laddered, with no more than $2 billion in unsecured debt coming due in any given year over the next decade. That’s why VICI enjoys BBB- credit ratings from S&P and Fitch with stable outlooks, as well as a Baa3 (BBB- equivalent) credit rating from Moody’s with a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to VICI’s Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and VICI’s March 2026 Investor Presentation).

Attractive Upside to Fair Value

Since December, VICI’s shares have returned 4%. Over that same time, the S&P 500 index (SP500) was flat. Even after this modest rally, the net lease REIT looks to be as compelling a value now as it was four months ago.

Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 11.4, which is in line with where the valuation was in December. For more perspective, this is much less than the eight-year average P/AFFO ratio of 15.3.

Moving forward, I believe that a reasonable fair value multiple for VICI is around 14. That would be nearly one standard deviation below the average P/AFFO ratio.

This accounts for the fact that VICI’s growth profile looks to be intact. That’s evidenced by the net lease REIT’s 3% to 4% annual AFFO per share growth consensus.

The only factor preventing VICI from fully returning to its eight-year average P/AFFO ratio is the interest rate environment. The 4.3% 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) is much higher than the 2.9% average over the past eight years, per data from Macrotrends. This is how I arrive at my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.

Currently, the calendar year 2026 is 27% in the books. That means another 73% of 2026 and 27% of 2027 are still ahead within the coming 12 months. This produces a forward 12-month AFFO per share input of $2.47.

Plugging my fair value P/AFFO multiple of 14 into this input, I compute a fair value of $35 a share. Against the current $28 share price (as of April 10th, 2026), this is equivalent to a 19% discount to fair value.

If VICI matches growth forecasts and reverts to fair value, it could post a 31% total return (32% annualized) by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, the net lease REIT could deliver 11% annual total returns.

The Payout Can Keep Rising

VICI’s 6.4% forward dividend yield is approximately 150 basis points higher than the 4.9% median forward yield of the real estate sector. That represented a 10 basis point narrowing of the spread since December. This is how VICI earns respective B+ and A- grades from Seeking Alpha’s Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (both of which were unchanged from December).

Since its IPO, VICI has raised its payout each year. That gives it a 7-year dividend growth streak, which is well above the sector median of 1.8 years. This is why the Quant System issues a B+ grade for overall dividend consistency (up from a B grade in December).

The net lease REIT’s payout is also reasonably safe. That’s supported by an AFFO payout ratio poised to be in the mid-70% range for 2026. Per the Zen Research Terminal, this would be better than the 90% payout ratio set forth by rating agencies for the industry.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the probability of a payout cut at 1.3% within the next 12 months. In the next average recession, this rises to 4.5%. That’s why I believe the A- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety is just about on the money (up from a B+ grade in December).

The Quant System anticipates 3.8% forward dividend per share growth from VICI. For more context, this is moderately above the sector median of 2.7%. This accounts for the Quant System’s B- grade for overall dividend growth (down from a B grade in December).

Risks to Consider

While VICI is set up for continued AFFO per share growth, it does face risks that could alter the investment thesis.

The key risk to the net lease REIT is its tenant concentration. VICI derived almost three-quarters of its cash rent (73%) from Caesars Entertainment (CZR) (39%) and MGM Resorts International (MGM) (34%). If either tenant faces a credit downgrade, VICI’s stock would be collateral damage in such a scenario.

Fortunately, VICI’s recent series of deals shows that it is laser-focused on broadening and further diversifying its tenant base. In the meantime, the most pressing risk to the net lease REIT is the ongoing lease negotiations with Caesars for its Regional Master Lease, which makes up around a quarter of VICI’s net operating income. Any rent reductions or less favorable escalation terms could set a precedent that may spook investors across the entire portfolio.

Another concentration risk to VICI is its dependence on Las Vegas. As of December 31st, 2025, 48% of the net lease REIT’s cash rent was derived from this market (the remaining 51% was mostly regional and slightly international in Alberta, Canada).

To be clear, I’m not all too worried about the experiential draw of Las Vegas itself (the city has arguably become the entertainment capital of the world). I believe the transformation of Las Vegas into a higher-end market continues to play out.

A risk worth watching tied to this geographic concentration is that the snow water equivalent for the western U.S. was the lowest on record for April 1st. The amount of water stored in western U.S. snowpack is nearly two-thirds (65%) below the 1991-2020 normal. How does this matter for VICI?

It doesn’t matter so much from an indoor operations perspective at its casinos. That’s because almost all water used indoors is treated and returned to Lake Mead, which means net consumption is almost zero.

The water used for outdoor features (fountains, lush landscaping, and pools) is emblematic of Las Vegas itself, and it’s almost completely consumptive. If Lake Mead falls below Tier 2 or Tier 3 levels, the Southern Nevada Water Authority could mandate stricter cuts in water consumption. That could force tenants to spend significant amounts of money to retrofit cooling systems or replace grass with turf, which could put further pressure on their rent coverage ratio.

Summary: A SWAN Holding for Me

VICI’s embedded organic growth through contractual escalations provides a growth tailwind for the net lease REIT. As do its acquisitions, like the Golden Entertainment sale-leaseback, expected to close in mid-2026, and its pending acquisition of a Canadian portfolio from Pure Casino Entertainment. The strategy to act as a capital provider for developments not only generates interest income for VICI but also opens the door to eventually acquiring the underlying real estate. The net lease REIT also boasts an investment-grade balance sheet.

Clinching my Buy case, VICI’s shares are as undervalued now as they were in December. This paves the way for 11% annual total returns for the foreseeable future, which is why I’m comfortable holding VICI as my portfolio’s 10th largest position, with a 2.2% weighting.

data.title: Jackson Financial: A Safe Name Among Private Credit Fears
data.description:
Shares of Jackson Financial (JXN) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining over 40%, as strong results have enabled material capital returns. That said, like much of the insurance sector, the stock has struggled more recently, down 20% from its high, as investors contemplate potential private credit losses in portfolios. While this is an important concern, Jackson is actually quite well positioned for potential challenges in this asset class, in my view. I have been a long-term bull on JXN, reiterating shares as a “strong buy” in January following its TPG (TPG) partnership; however, the stock is down 13% since then. With updated financials and recent developments, now is a good time to revisit JXN to see if this pullback is an opportunity.

In recent months, we have seen cracks in private credit build. This has led to $20 billion of redemption requests over the past quarter, and many firms have gated redemptions, given the fact most funds have a cap of 5% per quarter. Private credit, especially direct lending, has taken off over the past 15 years as banks rebuilt capital following the financial crisis and faced new regulations. This created the space for asset managers to lend directly to businesses that still sought loans.

Private credit is not innately more dangerous than public credit, so long as asset managers underwrite loans as rigorously as banks did. However, whenever an asset class grows dramatically, one should be wary that this growth is facilitated by easing lending standards, and direct lending has grown massively. There has been particular concern about software lending, especially with potential business disruption risk from AI. We have now seen several notable defaults where recovery was $0, which has added to alarm.

This is particularly relevant for the insurance industry because insurers are significant owners of private credit. Because their liabilities are long-dated, they can have less liquid portfolios, which increases the attractiveness of private vs. public markets. Plus, particularly in less risky policies (like fixed annuities), insurers make money not on the underwriting, but by having a higher investment yield. With private equity firms like KKR (KKR) and Apollo (APO) owning their own insurance companies, this has increased competition to enhance portfolio yields to remain competitive, requiring more private credit usage.

While a meaningful wave of defaults in private credit (which may or may not ultimately happen) would impact the insurance sector as a whole, not every insurer has a similar amount of exposure. In fact, Jackson had no material exposure to middle market or direct lending before a recently announced TPG partnership. Instead, to the extent it has private credit exposure, it is primarily investment-grade tranches of structured products, with Jackson historically relying on other asset classes, like emerging markets, to enhance its portfolio yield. As of the fourth quarter, it maintained a very conservative portfolio.

Within its corporate portfolio, less than 2% are rated below investment grade. Of its $4.4 billion ABS portfolio, 85% are rated A or higher, and just 1% are below investment grade. Its biggest allocation within this slice of its portfolio is in collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), which are structured securities supported by a portfolio of leveraged loans. Here, Jackson is defensively positioned with 93% of the portfolio rated-A or higher and 22% AAA. No AAA CLO has ever defaulted, given its structural seniority. I expect that to remain the case this cycle, and I see very little risk of JXN facing impairment on its CLO portfolio.

As a reminder, in January, Jackson signed an agreement with TPG whereby TPG invested $500 million in Jackson stock, and this deal was completed in February. Under the terms of the deal, JXN is receiving $150 million of TPG stock, so essentially it sold $500 million of stock for $650 million, or a 30% premium. With these proceeds, Jackson is able to seed a new captive reinsurance entity, Hickory Re, with $650 million to reinsure fixed and fixed index annuities. This enhances the capital efficiency of new sales inside its Jackson National Life subsidiary, making it easier to grow the business.

Now, TPG is paying a premium because it will begin investing a sleeve of Jackson’s portfolio. JXN will give TPG $4 billion within 2 years, ramping to $12 billion within 5 years. The contract lasts for five years. This will generate at least $60 million of annual management fees for TPG, which is how it aims to earn back the premium it is functionally paying for JXN’s shares. For Jackson, I view the initial financial terms as attractive. Absent this deal, it would have $500 million less to spend on buybacks (given the capital need for its new reinsurer). This year, when it buys back $500 million of stock, it will have the same share count as before TPG, a new reinsurance entity, and $150 million of TPG stock that it previously did not.

In essence, Jackson’s direct lending and private credit capabilities lagged peers, which could limit the ability to attractively price new policies. As a result, it is partnering with TPG to catch up. Given the fact that outstanding private credit is a source of concern, its lack of exposure is actually a positive, as JXN does not have this risk on its balance sheet.

Now, over time, as the TPG partnership ramps up, it will have more private credit exposure. However, if there is a time to invest in private credit, I would actually argue we are approaching that time. In periods of stress and outflows, there is a chance to buy assets at discounted prices, and if anything, underwriting rigor is likely to be stronger going forward than in the past. Plus, JXN is moving methodically, with only about 5% of its portfolio going to TPG this year.

Ultimately, being behind peers worked out in JXN’s favor, and it should face much lower credit losses than peers should a downturn occur. As such, any sell-off related to private credit fears is an opportunity in my view. This is especially the case because the company is performing at a high level. Looking back to recent results, Jackson delivered a blow-out fourth quarter, earning $6.61, which was $0.75 ahead of consensus.

Retail annuity sales hit a quarterly record in Q4 of $5.9 billion, and 2025 was also the strongest reported since JXN became a public company. In total, sales approached $20 billion. RILA sales were $2.3 billion in Q4 (up 53%) as the company launched an updated offering. Its RILA account values have reached $20 billion, up 14% from last year.

In total, nonvariable annuity flows were a positive $2.8 billion in the quarter, consistent with the company’s long-term strategy of derisking its liabilities. These policies are much less risky (especially relative to historic variable annuity offerings), and the steady growth in these products is reducing the overall riskiness of Jackson’s liabilities, which should pave the way for steadier capital returns. In contrast, variable annuity sales were up a modest 1% to $2.8 billion, and these sales continue to mix to less-risky offerings that do not provide lifetime guarantees. Over time, this should unlock potential multiple expansion, though I do not expect that to occur in a meaningful way until private credit overhangs from the sector are worked through.

Jackson Life maintains a stellar capital position with a risk-based capital ratio of 567%, well in excess of the 400-450% I like to see. With this capital posture, JXN can fund ongoing growth in annuity sales while continuing to dividend capital up to the parent holding company. These intercompany dividends are essential because it is the holding company that does share buybacks and pays the dividend. In 2025, Jackson’s holding company had $838 million of free cash flow, thanks to $1.1 billion of dividends from the operating company, less holding company expenses. The holding company also ended the year with $654 million of cash, in excess of the $500 million I like to see it hold.

During 2025, it repurchased $634 million of stock, reducing its share count by more than 8%. Plus, in February, Jackson also increased its dividend by 13% to $0.90, and shares now offer a 3.5% dividend yield. I expect JXN to continue growing capital returns thanks to durable cash flow and its strong financial positioning. Indeed, alongside Q4 results, JXN provided full-year guidance, expecting at least $1.2 billion of free capital generation alongside $900 million to $1.1 billion of capital returns.

Back in January, I was targeting $700-$750 million of buybacks and about $13.50/share of total capital returns, which is broadly consistent with guidance. That drove my fair value estimate of $135, using a 10x multiple. I still expect buybacks to end up in this area, and capital returns should be at least at that $13.50 level. With a safe investment portfolio and improving liability mix, I view this pace of capital return as sustainable. As such, I view the recent sell-off as an opportunity, and I continue to view JXN as a “strong buy.” I expect results this year to show its resilience to private credit challenges, and that should enable shares to rally, aided by the buyback. With over 30% upside, JXN is very attractive.

data.title: GE Vernova Isn't Priced For What It Has Already Become
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The absence of value has been experienced by the big-cap industrial groups all this time, with the most evident structural mispricing being GE Vernova (NYSE: GEV). The three business ventures that the company is currently undertaking are power, wind, and electrification; all three will undoubtedly benefit from two significant infrastructure build-outs of the present decade: the rise in the use of electricity through AI and the global energy transition. GEV has performed on all of its major Financial metrics—orders, backlog growth, revenue acceleration, margin expansion, and free cash flow generation—since its spin-off from General Electric in April 2024, and the market has continued to value the company using a traditional industrial OEM multiple to a business that is becoming more of a long-cycle infrastructure franchise with multi-year locked-in pricing and recurring services revenue.

The investment thesis is based on three pillars. First, the AI electricity supercycle is structural, not cyclical: U.S. data center power demand will increase 130% between 2024 and 2030, according to the IEA, and the five largest hyperscalers pledged to spend $660–690 billion in combined capital expenditure in 2026 alone—an infrastructure commitment that requires the specific equipment GEV manufactures. Second, GEV's $150 billion backlog entering 2026, combined with Slot Reservation Agreements (SRAs) priced 10-20 points above current backlog pricing, creates a compounding margin improvement that is built into future income statements but not yet visible in the current P&L. Third, the Prolec GE acquisition, completed on February 2, 2026, has made GEV the only company in the world capable of supplying an integrated 'power-to-rack' solution—from gas turbine to distribution transformer to switchgear—from a single supplier to hyperscale customers that are actively requesting exactly this kind of vertical integration.

Analysts covering GEV have a consensus 'Buy' rating based on 34 Wall Street analysts, with a median 12-month price target of $842.50 (ranging from $560 to $1,019), per Ticker Nerd analyst consensus data (April 2026). My independent price target of $1,150 per share reflects a 22x EV/EBITDA multiple on 2027 consensus EBITDA estimates of approximately $7.5–8 billion, a multiple appropriate for an infrastructure franchise with GEV's revenue visibility, free cash flow trajectory, and secular demand tailwinds. This represents approximately 28% upside from the current trading price.

The AI Electricity Supercycle: Why This Demand Is Structural

Although GEV may have an advantage in the data center, it is not the most appropriate way to render the magnitude of the structural change that is taking place in the U.S. electricity system. This concurrent growth of AI infrastructure, industrial electrification, and automation is a more sophisticated manner of expressing the matter. This was caused by the insufficiency in investment in transmission capacity, resulting in the greatest power generation and grid equipment demands since postwar electrification.

The scope of the problem is also established with the help of third-party data, which are credible. The International Energy Agency's Energy and AI report predicts that U.S. data center electricity consumption will grow by approximately 240 TWh between 2024 and 2030. As estimated by the Pew Research Center, the electricity consumption of U.S. data centers was more than 4% of total national electricity consumption, with 183 TWh used in 2024 and 426 TWh estimated by 2030. S&P Global Market Intelligence has estimated that in 2025, U.S. data center grid power demand will be 61.8 GW, and by 2030 it will be 134.4 GW—a figure never before realized on a large-scale basis.

Another sign of the imminence of the need to invest is the capital distribution of the hyperscalers. Futurum Group's AI Capex 2026 analysis estimates that the top five U.S. hyperscalers have already committed $660–690 billion in CapEx for 2026. In 2024, the U.S. electric utility sector alone had an expenditure of about $160 billion on the improvement of its infrastructure in terms of generation, transmission, and distribution. The technology companies are now outspending the utility industry on energy infrastructure by a factor of more than two to one.
