This has seen the lead time of power transformers increase to 128 weeks. According to Goldman Sachs, data center power demand across the world will increase by 165% by 2030. To fulfill this demand, GEV manufactures equipment that encompasses heavy-duty gas turbines to provide dispatchable baseload power, large transformers to interconnect the grid, and switchgear and substations to provide distribution, as well as HVDC systems for long-distance bulk transfer of power.

This demand cycle renders supply-side substitution virtually impossible, in contrast to most infrastructure boom and bust cycles. The outcome is an oligopoly of firms—GEV, Siemens Energy, and Mitsubishi Power—that manufacture heavy-duty gas turbines with combined-cycle efficiency exceeding 60%, encompassing virtually all the meaningful supply. Specialized metallurgy, precision casting, and prolonged qualification programs with utility operators are required in the manufacturing process. It is a process that new entrants are not able to accelerate. The establishment of additional capacity cannot be achieved in a few months or even a year. Owing to this, the backlog has grown from approximately $100 billion to $150 billion, and management estimates this will amount to ~$200 billion by 2028. The constraint is not demand; the constraint is supply.

The $150 Billion Backlog: Revenue Visibility That Has No Industrial Precedent

During the Q4 2025 earnings call, GE Vernova CEO Scott Strazik demonstrated that the company entered 2026 with a $150 billion backlog—50% larger than the backlog that existed when the GEV spin-off from General Electric was announced in November 2021. The increase in backlog, which stands at approximately 11% per year, has been accompanied by quality improvement, not just volume growth. Between 2025 and 2026, the equipment margin in backlog has gone up by six points, and management has guided a cumulative total of at least $22 billion in equipment margin additions to backlog between 2023 and 2026. GEV is not only accumulating orders at better prices than the current P&L reflects but is also reserving a margin profile materially higher than what is currently visible in the income statement.

The majority of the market commentary does not understand the significance of differentiating between firm backlog and SRAs, and it is the key to the power of forward earnings inherent in the order book. Firm orders are purchase commitments that are binding with fixed delivery dates and fixed prices. SRAs are lower-level commitments where customers place a deposit and commit to a pricing model on an earlier basis and then convert the commitment into firm orders, usually 6–18 months later. As of Q4 2025, GEV held approximately 40 GW of gas turbine firm orders and 43 GW of SRAs—totaling 83 GW of committed or reserved capacity against a current annualized production rate of roughly 16 GW and a mid-2026 target of 20 GW. The pricing on new SRAs is running 10–20 percentage points above the pricing embedded in existing firm backlog. As SRAs convert to firm orders and new SRAs are signed at current market rates, the margin profile of the backlog improves automatically—a self-reinforcing mechanism that management has already confirmed will add 'at least as many equipment margin dollars in backlog in 2026 as in 2025.

For investors who focus on revenue recognition mechanics, GEV does not book the full value of a gas turbine contract as revenue when the order is signed. Revenue is recognized as equipment is manufactured and delivered, typically 18–36 months after contract signature for large, heavy-duty turbines. The $150 billion backlog at year-end 2025 therefore represents contractually committed future revenue—revenue the income statement has not yet seen. With 2025 revenue of $38.1 billion, the backlog-to-revenue coverage ratio stands at 3.9x, implying that GEV has more than three and a half years of revenue fully covered even in a zero-new-orders scenario. That scenario is not happening: Q4 2025 orders totaled $22.2 billion—a book-to-bill ratio of approximately 2x—and management expects to reach approximately 100 GW under total contract by the end of 2026.

Power, Electrification, and the Prolec Catalyst

The three segments of GEV—power, wind, and electrification—each need to be analyzed separately because the risk/reward profile is different across the same corporate structure. A well-known headwind to the industry is due to the combination of U.S. offshore wind stop-work orders and onshore permitting difficulties, which would decrease wind revenue by the low double digits in 2026, with approximately $400 million in segment EBITDA losses. The most effective method to consider wind is that it is a contained and, to a large extent, a shielded drag—and therefore, any possible stabilization or recovery is a good upside optionality that fits into the zero current consensus model.

All this is fueled by the profitability of the power segment. Full-year 2025 Power orders increased by 52% on an organic basis, with 41 heavy-duty gas turbines and 18 aeroderivative units being delivered in Q4 alone. Management anticipates achieving organic growth of 16–18% in Power by 2026 with EBITDA margins also in the 16–18% range—a combination of volume leverage due to the production ramp, improved pricing on new contracts, and productivity improvements already in place in the current manufacturing base. GEV believes it can achieve an annualized gas turbine production of 20 GW by mid-2026 from its current run rate of approximately 16 GW, rising further to 24 GW by 2028. This rise in production that ships against backlog contracts at prevailing prices will result in more revenue and higher segment margin.

The electrification segment is probably the longest and most protracted pillar of the thesis. Revenue grew 32% year-over-year in Q4 2025, with EBITDA margin widening from 13.0% to 17.1%—rapidly approaching the profitability of the Power segment. Q4 2025 orders ran at approximately 2.5x revenue, building its own multi-year backlog base at an accelerating pace. Critically, data centers are now coming directly to GEV as co-creators of the power-to-rack solution, as CEO Strazik noted at the October 2025 Q3 earnings call.

The Prolec GE acquisition, completed February 2, 2026, for $5.275 billion, is the most strategic action that the company has made as a standalone public company. Prolec GE is a large transformer manufacturer with approximately 10,000 employees, seven manufacturing plants in the Americas, and five in the United States. Power transformers are currently the bottleneck in the AI infrastructure supply chain, with a lead time of 128 weeks. The initial acquisition of Prolec by GEV resulted in a conservative estimate of $3 billion in electrification segment revenue added to 2026 guidance, immediately accretive before any operational synergies, and positioned it as the only supplier capable of meeting hyperscaler demands with integrated grid solutions across the full voltage spectrum.

Valuation Analysis: The OEM Framework Versus the Infrastructure Franchise

Although the corporate structure of the organization is similar, the risk/reward profiles within the power, wind, and electrification sectors of GEV will vary, and different analytical methods will be required. Wind is probably a general headwind, and it is a familiar deficiency. The offshore wind stop-work orders issued by the U.S. will become permanent, and the onshore permitting also has concerns. This will cause Wind revenue to drop to the low double digits by 2026, with approximately $400 million in segment EBITDA losses. Think of Wind as a closed and shielded drag—any stabilization or recovery will be an upside on the zero current consensus model.

GEV's forward valuation cannot be assessed correctly using traditional industrial OEM multiples. It is better characterized by multi-year backlogs, SRA pricing, a global installed base, and co-development deals with hyperscalers. A long-cycle franchise that generates cash predictably is more comparable to an infrastructure investor's framework than to a cyclical equipment manufacturer that is subject to late-cycle order decay. The market is not asking the right question—whether 39x 2026 EV/EBITDA is high for an industrial is not the issue. What should be asked is, what should a $150 billion locked-demand infrastructure franchise with a 20% EBITDA margin target by 2028 and $24 billion of committed free cash flow be worth against the 2027–2028 earnings base?

GEV currently trades at approximately 33x 2026 EV/EBITDA at the midpoint of guidance. This appears elevated against a traditional industrial peer set—but the comparison is structurally misleading. When the same enterprise value is applied to the 2028 EBITDA target of 20% on $56 billion revenue—implying approximately $11.2 billion of EBITDA—the implied 2028 EV/EBITDA falls to approximately 20x on a business growing EBITDA by more than 100% from 2026 levels under management-confirmed guidance. A 20x multiple is hardly aggressive for a company with earnings growth anchored to contractually committed backlog margins of 16–18% in power and 17–19% in electrification.

Risk Factors

Offshore Wind Policy and Execution Drag

The most prominent danger is the wind sector. The impact of the work order suspension issued by the current administration on offshore wind in the U.S. is, however, significant. The offshore wind development plans of the company's business in the U.S. are anticipated to incur EBITDA losses of up to $400 million. The management appears to be control-oriented, but the policy front is not clear, and in case the administration were to extend the offshore wind restrictions to cover international projects or enable onshore permitting, the wind would still drag. The risk is partly offset by the wind losses that have already been factored into guidance conservatism and the development of indications of the revival in U.S. onshore wind repowering: in 2025, GEV's Onshore Wind division was assigned the responsibility of repowering 1.1 GW of onshore turbines.

Gas Turbine Production Ramp Timing

The most important operational milestone in a shorter time period and applicable to any investment thesis is the goal of generating 20 GW per year. The risk of execution in capital-intensive industries is caused by the availability of skilled labor, constraints of the supply chain of specialized alloys, and equipment qualification timelines, among others. The potential revenue recorded on Power backlogs may fall substantially between mid-2026 and Q4 2026 or early 2027, causing missed quarterly earnings. The risk of late deliveries is mostly self-remedying—provided the demand does not decrease faster than the supply, revenue is deferred but not lost.

Tariff and Trade Policy Exposure

The manufacturing operations of the company are evenly distributed across North America, Europe, and Asia, and in case of an increase in industrial tariffs, it would incur higher costs of acquiring specialized materials such as steel and copper. Crucial mitigants include improved domestic content from its five U.S. manufacturing sites and price escalation provisions in long-term contracts of Prolec GE. As indicated in the Deloitte analysis of U.S. AI infrastructure, developed economies are more concerned with grid infrastructure than with national security, which has resulted in the establishment of tariff systems that penalize manufacturers of energy equipment who profit from home-based income.

Capital Allocation: A Company That Has Changed Its DNA

Nonetheless, the constantly reiterated capital allocation framework that GEV has articulated and executed is a definite sign that their leadership seized the chance of value creation. The company repurchased over 8 million shares and returned $3.6 billion in 2025 while simultaneously funding the $5.3 billion Prolec GE acquisition and investing in gas turbine production capacity expansion across its French and South Carolina facilities. A $200 million investment in a new electrification manufacturing facility has been initiated in Hai Phong, Vietnam. The board raised the share repurchase authorization to $10 billion from the previously approved $6 billion program and doubled the quarterly dividend to $0.50 per share, with the company ending the year with a cash balance of $8.8 billion. The cumulative free cash flow commitment of at least $24 billion from 2025 to 2028—against a current market capitalization of approximately $242 billion—implies GEV will generate roughly 10% of its current market cap per year in free cash flow over the next three years, a metric that is simply not reflected in the current multiple.

The fact that gross leverage has been kept below 1x adjusted EBITDA even after the financing of the $5.3 billion Prolec acquisition—funded equally with cash and new debt—is the opposite of an industrial company trying to adopt similar growth opportunities. The positive balance sheet of the spin will help in accelerating strategic flexibility in case of additional M&A opportunities in grid infrastructure, which will consolidate with time as transformer and switchgear manufacturers acquire strategic benefits in the field of AI power build-out. When the dividend and buyback plan is continued, the strength of GEV can grow. Management has explicitly committed to returning at least one-third of cash generation to shareholders through higher dividends and the increased buyback program—which will produce a floor under the stock price as the FCF trajectory builds.

Bull and Bear Cases

Bull Case: $1,400+ Price Target

The electrification margins will start moving towards the upper end of the range of 17–19% as soon as GEV achieves its production target of mid-2026. The synergies brought about by the acquisition of Prolec GE will be brought to the fore in the Q2 2026 earnings call. When 2027 EBITDA revisions become more than $8.5 billion, the SRA pricing step-up will be reflected in literal sell-side estimates—forcing a rerating into 25× 2027 EBITDA after 2027 EBITDA revisions. And any partial fix to U.S. offshore wind policy headwinds, such as the suspension of the expansion of stop-work orders, would open the door to the eradication of the estimated $400 million a year wind drag by 2027. The value of the enterprise will be about $212 billion, assuming a 25× multiple on $8.5 billion of EBITDA in 2027. GEV has a total debt of about $1.2 billion and cash of about $8.8 billion, with a net cash position of about $7.6 billion. With a diluted stock outstanding of 270 million shares, adding the net cash position to the enterprise value would translate to an equity value of about $219.6 billion, or $1,400 per share (≈ $219.6 billion ÷ 270 million shares).

Bear Case: $650 Price Target

Two interdependent headwinds exist: the first is that the production ramp of gas turbines can be prolonged to late 2026 or early 2027, pushing Power segment revenue below the 16–18% organic growth guidance, and the second is that the U.S. tariff policy will raise the cost of inputs beyond the reimbursable range of the contract escalation clauses, narrowing the EBITDA margins toward the lower end of the 11–13% guidance range. In this case, the 2026 EBITDA would be around $4.5 billion, at the low end of the guidance-implied range, and the market would be beginning to attach a multiple compression of between 25× and 27× as it becomes increasingly clear that execution issues are arising. At 26× EBITDA of $4.5 billion, equity value is approximated at about $124.6 billion, and adding the net cash position of about $7.6 billion to the $117 billion implied enterprise value (26× × $4.5 billion EBITDA) results in a value of about $650 per share (≈ $124.6 billion ÷ 270 million shares). The bear case is unrealistic without both execution and policy headwinds failing at the same time—none of these risks by themselves is sufficient to achieve such a result.

Conclusion: The Power Grid Is The Underpriced AI Infrastructure Layer

The IEA estimates U.S. data center electricity consumption to grow by 130% by 2030. In 2026, the capital expenditure on hyperscalers was between $660 billion and $690 billion, and this corresponds to over four (4) years of the annual spending on infrastructure of the U.S. utility industry. The Microsoft CEO has made it public that they are unable to connect the GPUs with the grid, and this has led to them being held up in stocks. It is not a bottleneck of computation. The answer to the bottleneck is GE Vernova, with a backlog of $150 billion, 83 GW of contracted gas turbine projects, and the only integrated power-to-rack electrification platform in the world.

It is not a prediction of the Financial situation—it is a statement. According to management guides for 2026, adjusted EBITDA margins of 11–13% and revenue of between $44 billion and $45 billion are anticipated. The current 3.9x annual revenues backlog, with free cash flow of $5.0–$5.5 billion, reflects prices currently 10–20 pts higher than what is visible in the income statement. The management has committed to providing $56 billion in revenue, 20% EBITDA margins, and $24 billion in cumulative free cash flow by 2028. These are not the projections of hedged forward analysts but targets enhanced with more difficult customer commitments at higher prices. The next verification will occur on April 22 with the Q1 2026 earnings call, and it is clear that each successive quarter the ramp turns into a new quarter, making it even harder to rationalize the multiple currently in place.

My 12-month price forecast of $1,150 per share, implying 22x 2027 EBITDA, is up by a respectable 28% from current prices, yet still by far the lowest among analyst estimates on an equal basis. I regard shares as having limited upside because of what I believe to be impractical operations enhancement. The bear case—involving production delays, tariff exposure, and a long WIND drag—has already been taken care of with deliberately conservative management guidance. Such structural elements as SRA conversions at 10–20 point margin premiums, Prolec synergy recognition, and wind segment recovery optionality are absent, which is a peculiarity of the bull case. The backlog of $150 billion printing at 20% EBITDA margins should give investors a clear picture of what is in store. The large-cap value proposition in the AI infrastructure cycle is the one that is not in line with the current prices of GE Vernova, and this is what makes it a viable option.

data.title: Ingles Markets' Surge Doesn't Mean Its Discount Is Gone
data.description:
The last few months have been an exceptional time for shareholders of Ingles Markets (IMKTA). Since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate back in January of this year, shares have skyrocketed 28.9%. That is significantly better than the 7.3% drop that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. The company has benefited from a surge in revenue, profits, and cash flows, even though it continues to struggle with some of its locations being closed down because of hurricane-related weather events. The market seems to finally be recognizing just how cheap the stock is. In fact, in my last article, I made the case that the discount that the business was trading at in relation to other similar firms would not last. And sure enough, I have been right so far.

The way I view it, some of the easiest upside has already been captured in recent months. But that doesn't mean that investors should sell. The company is much more favorable from a risk standpoint than other similar enterprises. It's also trading cheaper than most of them, especially when it comes to its price-to-book multiple. Long term, I expect performance to continue to impress. And because of that, I will maintain my ‘strong buy’ rating on the stock.

Still selling for cheap

The only new fundamental data that investors have regarding Ingles Markets covers the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company came in strong at $1.37 billion. That is up 6.6% over the $1.29 billion that the company reported a year earlier. This surge in revenue came in spite of the fact that the company suffered to the tune of $2.1 million from the fact that three of its locations remain closed as a result of Hurricane Helene, which decimated them back in September of 2024. The overall rise in revenue for the company, then, came from a surge in comparable store sales. That includes fuel. These revenues jumped $75.9 million year over year. That's an increase of 6.1%. But if we exclude fuel from the equation, the rise was even better at 6.2%. I wish I could say that we had more data on this to explain whether this was traffic, higher prices, or a mixture of the two. But unfortunately, management is quite opaque when it comes to reporting these figures.

Regardless, as revenue improved, profitability improved also. Net income shot up from $16.6 million to $28.1 million. Obviously, the higher revenue helped here. But that wasn't all. The firm saw its gross profit margin expand from 23.4% to 24.4%. Even without fuel costs, the company benefited to the tune of 76 basis points when it came to gross profit margin. Operating and administrative costs, meanwhile, declined from 21.8% of revenue to 21.5%. This dip was because of non-fuel items such as salaries and wages, insurance, bank charges, and more. As net profits improved, other profitability metrics for the company improved also. Operating cash flow, as an example, went from negative $43.6 million to positive $38.4 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get a rise from $45.2 million to $55.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $50.4 million to $74.8 million.

One thing that certainly helped here was the fact that the hurricane related impact that the company saw is now a distant memory. Even though revenue continues to suffer because those stores remain closed, all of the costs associated with those closures have largely become a thing of the past. Even the revenue impact is small compared to what it was previously. Management estimated, for instance, that during a three-week window of time in 2025, road and power outages prevented several of its stores from operating, costing it between $55 million and $65 million in revenue. But seeing as how the impact for the most recent quarter was only $2.1 million, it's clear that the storm has passed.

One of the great things about the company, or perhaps the best thing about it, is the fact that shares are incredibly cheap. If we project out financial performance based on what we saw during the first quarter of 2026, we would get net profits for the year of $141.5 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $258.4 million, while EBITDA would come in at $357.5 million. Using these estimates, and also using the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced and how they stack up against five other similar enterprises. Focusing on the 2025 multiples, the firm is not the cheapest, but it's certainly not pricey either. Only one of the competitors is cheaper than it is on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. Two of them are cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, while three of them are cheaper in relation to earnings.

This is not the only lens through which we should view the business, though. In the chart above, as an example, you can see how the company looks from a price-to-book standpoint. This is perhaps the most striking valuation difference that I see. It is, by far, cheaper than these other comparable firms. In fact, if it were to trade at the price-to-book multiple of the cheapest of these competitors, that would imply upside for shareholders of 169.3%. And if it were to trade at the average, that would mean an upside of 412.3%. The price-to-sales multiple is not as impressive, but it's still quite impressive all the same. In the chart below, you can see that only one of the companies that I compared it to ended up being cheaper in this regard. All of the rest are more expensive, though not as expensive when it comes to the price-to-book approach.

Normally when you see a business trade at a significant discount like this, it's because of either declining financial performance, which we clearly do not see here, or high amounts of debt that make the company risky. That doesn't appear to be the case either. In the chart below, you can see the company's net leverage ratio compared to the net leverage ratio of its five competitors. None of them come even close. In fact, if Ingles Markets wanted to borrow enough money that it would have the same net leverage ratio as the lowest leveraged of the five competitors, that would mean it would have to grow its $152.1 million of net debt by another $270.4 million. This could certainly give the company fuel with which to grow further. But management has decided to remain principled in how they approach operations.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that is at our disposal right now, I continue to be shocked at just how cheap Ingles Markets happens to be. The company is insanely attractive from a valuation standpoint. That's especially true on a price-to-book and price-to-sales basis. The risk profile is low, and management has clearly demonstrated some strong performance in the most recent quarter. When you add all of this together, it makes for a very compelling ‘strong buy’ pick in my opinion.
